title: Carved cherry dessert server
subtitle: Wide-bowled cherry spoon for serving soft puddings and fruit
excerpt: A dessert server with a wide, shallow bowl and a curved neck that angles the bowl for ease of serving from a deep dish. Cherry carves beautifully and takes a linseed finish with a warm reddish tone. The angled neck distinguishes this from a standard eating spoon; the angle is achieved by offsetting the bowl axis during rough shaping.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885); Florence White Good Things in England (1932) references carved serving spoons as a standard kitchen utensil.

content.text: Cherry is the treen wood for pieces that will be displayed as well as used. It dries to a warm reddish-brown, deepening with oil and age; the surface is fine and close, and it takes a sharper detail than ash or sycamore. A dessert server needs a wider, shallower bowl than an eating spoon: the wider bowl loads more of a soft pudding per scoop, and the angled neck holds the bowl horizontal when the handle is tilted toward the serving dish. The angled neck is the defining feature and the part that takes the most care in the rough-shaping stage.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: strop every fifteen minutes. Cherry is fine-grained and rewards a sharper edge than oak or ash.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
A
green
cherry
blank

: 24 cm long, 8 cm wide, 4 cm deep. Wild cherry (Prunus avium) and cultivated cherry are both usable. Tree surgeons dealing with orchard trees are a reliable source. The heartwood of cherry is the warm reddish-brown; the sapwood at the edges is paler. A blank that includes both heart and sapwood will show a contrast once oiled; either accept this as a feature or select a blank from the heartwood side.
Method

Draw the profile: handle 16 cm, angled neck 2 cm (the bowl axis angled 15 degrees from the handle axis), bowl oval 6 cm long and 4.5 cm wide. The angled neck is drawn by tilting the bowl oval off the straight centreline rather than bending the handle; the straight handle makes the server easy to grip.
Rough the profile with the carving axe if available, or with
push cuts

on the sloyd knife alone if the axe is not to hand. The angled neck means more material to remove on one side of the neck than the other; take care not to cut past the pencil line on the thinner side.
Shape the neck angle with
thumb-pivot cuts

: small controlled arcs through the transition from handle axis to bowl axis. Take off no more than 1 mm per pass at the neck; the neck is narrower than the handle and the grain runs at an angle through the transition.

Hollow the bowl with the medium hook knife. The bowl is shallow; target 1.5 cm depth at the deepest point. A wide shallow bowl takes more hook-knife passes at a lower angle than a deep narrow one; the blade barely curves into the surface on each stroke.

Refine the outside of the bowl with push cuts. The back of the bowl should be gently convex, thinning smoothly toward the rim. Check: hold the spoon horizontally; the bowl face should be roughly parallel to the floor when the handle is at the angle of a relaxed grip.
Leave to dry 2 to 3 weeks. Cherry dries without much tendency to split if the walls are even.
Finishing
Sand through 120, 180, and 220 grit; cherry's fine grain rewards the extra grit stage. Apply raw linseed oil; 2-week cure. The oil will darken the cherry to a rich warm brown.
Variations
Matched pair: carve a serving spoon and serving fork to the same handle profile and length; the angled neck matches on both.
Walnut: a darker and slightly heavier wood; the contrast against a pale ceramic serving dish is marked.
Left-handed version: the neck angle tilts to the other side; the bowl should sit horizontal in a left-handed serving grip.
Care
Wash with warm water; towel-dry at once. Cherry reacts to extended water exposure by softening the linseed film; re-oil when the surface looks dull.

items.fix: Leave the neck thicker than the final target until the bowl is hollowed. The angled neck is the last section to refine; it has no material left to absorb a slip. Read the grain through the transition before each cut.
items.cause: Grain running against the direction of cut through the angled neck; combined with a thin neck section
items.symptom: Neck snaps at the angle transition during thumb-pivot cuts

items.fix: Mark the neck angle with a protractor before cutting. The 15-degree angle is a useful starting point; adjust by half a degree if the bowl tips too far in use.
items.cause: The angled neck drawn too steeply or too shallowly during layout
items.symptom: Bowl does not sit horizontal when handle is at grip angle
